channel.title: Society on The Daily Thrum
channel.description: Recent content in Society on The Daily Thrum

item.title: Sydneysiders Campaign For Transport Industrial Action To Continue After Discovering Transport Still Runs But Opal Readers Are Off
item.description:
SYDNEY — Thousands of Sydneysiders have thrown their support behind ongoing Transport for NSW industrial action after discovering that many buses, trams and trains are still running, traffic remains broadly fucked, and the Opal readers are mysteriously switched off.

Commuters who had initially reacted to the disruption with frustration said their mood changed dramatically after boarding a bus, instinctively reaching for their Opal card, and being waved through by a driver radiating the calm authority of a man who no longer cares what happens to the revenue model.

item.title: Mistreated, Neglected Cat Has Not Been Petted For Over Ten Minutes
item.description:
SYDNEY — According to sources, a local cat is being cruelly neglected by his human after she allegedly failed to pat him for more than ten consecutive minutes.

Tubby domestic shorthair Tom Frampton issued the statement from the armrest after owner Carla Pickering briefly stopped stroking him in order to answer an email, drink water, and perform several other non-cat-related activities described by Frampton as “deeply troubling and infuriating”.

Witnesses say Frampton initially attempted to resolve the matter through established diplomatic channels, including staring, shifting his weight, placing one paw on Pickering’s forearm, and slowly extending a single claw into her sleeve.

item.title: Cosmote To Launch In Australia, Rebrands “What’s Up” Prepaid Tier As “Scarnon, Cunts”
item.description:
ATHENS/SYDNEY — Greek telecommunications giant Cosmote has announced plans to launch in Australia, confirming its popular “What’s Up” prepaid mobile tier will be localised for the Australian market as “Scarnon, Cunts”.

The rebrand follows months of consumer research, cultural consultation, and one marketing executive spending 11 minutes on a Sydney beach before declaring the strategy “basically done”.

“We understood that Americanisms may work in countries where English is a second language,” said regional spokesperson Thomas Framptonidis. “But Australians require a more authentic greeting architecture. You cannot enter this market saying ‘what’s up’ like an American teen.”
